comments:
Summary:
This paper proposes a set of principles intended to characterize what a formal propositional logic should satisfy to perform “plausible reasoning” (informal notions such as usually, typically, probably). It then defines a new propositional logic, Propositional Plausible Logic (PPL), including syntactic machinery (strict/defeasible/warning rules), multiple proof algorithms (factual, ambiguity-blocking, ambiguity-propagating variants), a proof relation, evaluation trees, and a proof function. The paper proves many meta-theoretic properties of PPL (decisiveness, various consistency properties, right weakening, conjunction/disjunction behavior, a truth-value mapping with at least three values) and demonstrates PPL on several canonical examples (n-lottery, ambiguity puzzle, non-monotonicity example). It also compares PPL against a wide range of existing non-monotonic approaches and argues that PPL uniquely satisfies all the proposed principles.

Strengths:
- Ambitious and well-organized: the paper clearly separates its two goals (principles and a logic satisfying them), and structures the material so the reader can follow the development.
- Thorough formal development: definitions, multiple equivalent characterizations (proof relation, trees, proof function), and a comprehensive set of theorems with proofs in appendices. The logic handles conjunction and disjunction in ways that address common pitfalls (e.g. lottery paradox) and explicitly treats warning/defeater rules.
- Conceptually original synthesis: proposing a coherent set of principles for plausibility-oriented logics and building a proof-theoretic system intended to meet them is valuable for the non-monotonic reasoning community.
- Good comparative discussion: the manuscript surveys many non-monotonic frameworks (Default Logic, circumscription, ASP, argumentation) and explains why they fail particular principles or examples; this helps situate PPL.

Weaknesses and suggestions for improvement:
- Readability and density: the formal sections are heavy with notation and definitions. While rigorous, the presentation could be made more accessible by adding more intuition after key definitions and by providing a few guiding, informal walkthroughs of the principal inference patterns before giving full formal definitions.
- Practical aspects underdeveloped: the paper notes an implementation exists but gives no details or empirical results. There is no complexity or computational-cost analysis of PPL proofs or evaluation trees. Readers will wonder about decidability/complexity for nontrivial rule sets, practical performance, and how the multiple algorithms affect efficiency.
- Guidelines for priorities and modelling choices: the priority relation on rules (>) is central but mostly treated abstractly. Practical heuristics or principled ways to derive > from knowledge sources (specificity, recency, authority) would improve usability.
- Examples are illustrative but small: the chosen examples (lotteries, ambiguity puzzle, simple contradiction) are canonical and effective; however larger, more realistic case studies would strengthen claims about applicability.
- Relationship to probabilistic / quantitative approaches: the introduction motivates the non-use of numeric degrees; a short discussion clarifying trade-offs, and when PPL is preferable to probabilistic methods, would be helpful. (This may be implicit in the principles, but could be made explicit.)
- Minor editorial issues: there are some repetitions and long paragraphs in the survey section; pruning or moving extended comparisons to an appendix could improve flow.

Soundness and novelty:
The paper gives a thorough formal treatment with many theorems and proofs; within the scope of this manuscript the system appears self-consistent and carefully argued. The claim that PPL is the only non-numeric, propositionally adequate logic satisfying all the given principles is supported by the comparative discussion, though the claim depends on the chosen set of principles; the paper is clear about that. Originality is good: the combination of an explicit principles list with a matching logic that preserves propositional expressivity and handles conjunction/disjunction is a useful contribution.

Recommendation to the author / next steps:
1) Add a short section or appendix describing the implementation, any algorithmic optimizations, and (if possible) small empirical results (even running times on synthetic rule sets) to convince readers of practicality. 2) Include a brief complexity/decidability discussion: under what conditions on R and > are evaluation trees guaranteed finite? The paper defines plausible theories to require finiteness of trees, but more discussion on syntactic conditions ensuring this would be useful. 3) Provide more modelling guidance for priorities and for choosing which proof algorithm to use in an application scenario. 4) Consider streamlining some of the dense formal parts with additional intuition and worked examples; a summary table contrasting PPL properties with major competing systems would help readers quickly appreciate differences.

Overall assessment:
This is a substantial, careful and original contribution to non-monotonic reasoning. It will be of interest to researchers working on defeasible inference, argumentation, and formal models of commonsense plausibility. With improved exposition and more attention to computational aspects, this work could have significant impact. I therefore recommend acceptance / positive consideration (see numeric recommendation).
